title: Chapter 29: The Deep Paths
content: The entrance to the Deep Paths was beneath Blackwood University.

Roxanne had walked past it a hundred times during her years as a student — an old maintenance door in the basement of the Fine Arts building, padlocked, labeled with a faded sign that read: AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY. She had never thought twice about it.
Now she knew why it was there.

"The Hollow Cathedral connects to the Deep Paths," Silas said, pulling the padlock off with a single twist. The metal groaned and snapped. "The university was built on a convergence point. The founders knew. That's why they chose this location."
"The founders of Blackwood University knew about the Nether-Grid?"
"Some of them were not entirely human."
"Of course they weren't."
She pushed the door open.
Beyond it, a staircase descended into darkness. Not the ordinary darkness of an unlit basement. This darkness had weight. Texture. It pressed against the eyes like a physical force.

"The Deep Paths don't obey the same rules as the surface Nether-Grid," Silas said. "Time flows differently. Distance is subjective. Entities here don't recognize contracts, debt, or the Exchange. They recognize power and nothing else."
"So basically I'm walking into a place where my card might not work."
"Your card will always work. It's the original contract. But the entities here may not care what a contract says."
Roxanne looked down the staircase.
"Then I'll have to be convincing."
She stepped forward.

The staircase went down for what felt like miles. The air grew cold. The violet light of the surface Nether-Grid faded, replaced by something darker — a blue-black luminescence that seemed to come from nowhere and everywhere at once.
Silas walked beside her, his silver hair catching the strange light. He had shed his Victorian formality. Here, in the Deep Paths, he moved differently. More alert. More dangerous.
"You're tense," Roxanne observed.
"I am cautious. There is a difference."
"You sound like Frost."
"I have been spending too much time around your accountant."
They reached the bottom of the staircase. The passage opened into a vast cavern.
The cavern had no walls. It extended in every direction, infinite and featureless, except for the Path — a ribbon of dark stone that wound through the void like a frozen river.
And on the Path, something waited.

It was tall. Taller than anything Roxanne had seen in the Nether-Grid. Its body was a column of obsidian, featureless except for a single vertical line running down its center — a seam, like an eyelid that never opened.
It had no face. No limbs. But it was facing them.
"A Watcher," Silas said quietly. "They guard the thresholds of the Deep Paths. They do not speak. They do not negotiate. They judge."
"Judge what?"
"Whether you are permitted to pass."
"And if I'm not?"
"Then you do not pass."
Roxanne looked at the Watcher. Then at the Path beyond it. Then at the black card in her hand.
"Can I buy it?"
"It does not recognize currency."
"Can I buy the Path?"
"The Path does not have an owner."
"So what does it recognize?"
Silas was quiet for a moment.
"Truth."
"Truth?"
"The Watchers predate the concept of deception. They were created in an era when lies did not exist. To pass one, you must speak something absolutely true. Anything less, and it will not move."
Roxanne looked at the Watcher.
"What if I don't know what's absolutely true?"
"Then you will stand here until you figure it out. Or until the Watcher decides you are not worth waiting for."
She took a breath.
Then she stepped forward.
The Watcher's seam shifted. The slightest movement — an acknowledgment that it had noticed her.

"I am Roxanne Vance," she said. "I am the heir of the Primordial Void. I hold the original contract. And I am here to open a trade route because seven ancient beings voted to isolate me instead of facing me directly."
She paused.
"That is true. All of it."
The Watcher was still.
Then, slowly, the vertical seam opened.
Not an eye. A doorway.
Beyond it, the Deep Path stretched into infinity.
The Watcher had moved aside.
Silas stared at her. "How did you know that would work?"
"I didn't. But I figured if the Watcher can detect lies, the safest thing to say is what actually happened."
"You told a Primordial artifact the unvarnished truth about your situation."
"It worked, didn't it?"
He looked at the open doorway. Then at her.
"You are terrifying."
"Thank you."
She stepped through.
Beyond the Watcher's doorway, the Deep Path unfolded like a dream.

Not a tunnel. Not a road. It was a corridor of possibility — every step took them through a different fragment of reality. One moment they walked through a forest of crystal trees. The next, across a bridge of woven shadow. The next, through the silent gallery of an abandoned city where statues wept black oil.
"The Deep Paths connect everything," Silas said. "Every abandoned dimension. Every forgotten realm. Every place the Nether-Grid couldn't reach."
"So it's a back-alley through the multiverse."
"A very dangerous back-alley."
They walked for what felt like hours. Time passed strangely here — sometimes fast, sometimes slow, sometimes not at all.
Then they found it.
A settlement.

It clung to the edge of the Path like a barnacle on a ship — a cluster of buildings made from salvaged materials, lit by braziers of cold flame. Figures moved between the structures. Not Collectors. Not debtors. Something else.
"Traders," Silas said. "Independent operators. They navigate the Deep Paths outside the Exchange's authority. They answer to no one."
"Can they be bought?"
"Everything can be bought. The question is whether their price is something you're willing to pay."
Roxanne looked at the settlement. At the cold flames. At the figures moving in the dark.
"Let's find out."
She stepped off the Path and walked toward the lights.
The settlement was called the Crossroads.

Not a formal name — just what the traders called it. A cluster of mismatched structures built from the wreckage of a hundred collapsed dimensions. Signs in languages Roxanne didn't recognize hung over doorways. The air smelled of ozone, burnt sugar, and something metallic.
A figure approached them. Humanoid. Four arms. Eyes that glowed faintly green.
"New faces," it said. Its voice was cracked, like old leather. "Fresh ones. You don't smell like Exchange."
"We're not Exchange."
"Good. Exchange never comes here. Too afraid of the Paths." It studied her. "What do you want?"
"Trade routes. Supplies. Reagents. Anything the Exchange won't sell me."
The figure laughed. A dry, rasping sound. "You want to compete with the Exchange?"
"I want to replace the Exchange."
The laughter stopped.
"That's ambitious for someone who smells like mortal."
Roxanne held up the black card.
The figure's green eyes widened.
"Is that —"
"The original contract. Yes."
"...You're her. The one who bought a Frost-bringer."
"Word travels fast."
"Word travels fastest in the dark."
The figure extended a hand. Three of its four arms crossed over its chest in what might have been a gesture of respect.
"I'm Kallos. I run the Crossroads. If you want supplies, I can get you supplies. But I don't work for free."
"I'm not asking you to work for free. I'm asking you to work for a better currency."
She produced an Infinity Note.
Kallos took it. Turned it over. The ∞ symbol caught the cold firelight.
"What backs this?"
"The same thing that backs the card. The Void bloodline. The original contract."
"The Exchange says your money is worthless outside your territory."
"The Exchange says a lot of things. Try spending that note at the dining hall in my territory and see what happens."
Kallos was silent for a long moment.
Then: "How many of these do you have?"
"Infinite."
"...Come inside. We'll talk."
Roxanne followed Kallos into the Crossroads, with Silas a step behind her.
The Deep Paths had delivered their first ally.
An hour later, Roxanne emerged from the Crossroads with three things:
A map of the Deep Paths — hand-drawn on cured demon-hide, showing routes the Exchange had never charted.
A list of independent suppliers — entities who traded outside the Exchange's jurisdiction and had no loyalty to the Council.
And a standing agreement with Kallos: Infinity Notes accepted at the Crossroads at face value, with a 2 percent conversion fee.
"Two percent is steep," Roxanne had said.
"Two percent is fair. I'm taking a risk accepting your money before anyone else does."
"You're taking a calculated risk. You saw the card. You know what it's worth."
"I know what it could be worth. There's a difference."
"There always is."
They had shaken hands — all four of Kallos's hands — and the deal was sealed.
Walking back through the Deep Paths, Silas was quiet for a long time.
Then: "You just opened a supply chain that bypasses the Exchange entirely."
"I opened a conversation. The supply chain comes next."
"You did it in one evening. With a handshake and a piece of paper."
"And an infinite card."
"And an infinite card. But the card doesn't negotiate. You do."
Roxanne looked at the map in her hands. The Deep Paths stretched across it like veins of dark light, connecting territories the Exchange had never touched.
"The Council voted to isolate me," she said. "They thought cutting off my trade routes would starve my economy. But they forgot something."
"What?"
"The Exchange isn't the only economy. It's just the biggest one. And the biggest ones are always the slowest to adapt."
She folded the map and tucked it into her pocket.
"By the time the Council realizes I've built my own supply chain, it'll be too late to stop me."
*End of Chapter 29*
